title: Saturated and Anisotropic Magnetostriction in an Altermagnet
authors: Zhiyuan Duan, Qiyun Xu, Peixin Qin, Li Liu, Guojian Zhao, Yuzhou He, Xiaoyang Tan, Sixu Jiang, Jingyu Li, Xiaoning Wang, Qinghua Zhang, Wenhui Duan, Yong Xu, Ziang Meng, Peizhe Tang, Chengbao Jiang, Zhiqi Liu
abstract: Magnetostriction, a fundamental phenomenon bridging magnetism and mechanics, has enabled a broad spectrum of applications. For almost two centuries, it has been mainly investigated for ferromagnets. Regarding the magnetostriction of antiferromagnets (AFMs), limitedly known examples for both conventional collinear AFMs and noncollinear AFMs predominantly exhibit non-saturating magnetic-field dependence. Herein, we report an easily saturated magnetostriction effect in a prototypical altermagnet - MnTe, which is an emerging class of collinear AFMs with special crystal symmetries. For high-quality MnTe single crystals, the magnetostriction saturates under a moderate field of ~0.7 T with an intriguing two-fold-symmetry anisotropy. First-principles calculations reveal that the saturated and anisotropic magnetostriction originates from symmetry-allowed coupling between elastic strain and its Néel order parameter. These findings break the traditional wisdom on antiferromagnetic magnetostriction.
summary: This work reports the discovery of a saturated and anisotropic magnetostriction effect in the altermagnet MnTe. By combining detailed magnetometry and strain measurements with first-principles calculations, the authors demonstrate that this effect arises from a symmetry-allowed coupling between strain and the Néel order parameter. This challenges traditional understanding of AFM magnetostriction and highlights altermagnets as promising materials for magnetostrictive applications.
main_problem: The study investigates the magnetostriction, a coupling between magnetism and mechanics, specifically in antiferromagnets (AFMs) and the emerging class of altermagnets.
main_result: The authors report observing a saturated and highly anisotropic magnetostriction in the altermagnet MnTe, which is theoretically linked to symmetry-allowed coupling between elastic strain and the Néel order parameter.
method: The research combines extensive experimental measurements (magnetostriction, magnetization, resistivity) with first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations and Landau theory analysis.
model_or_system: The physical system is the MnTe single crystal, which exhibits altermagnetic properties. The theoretical framework involves modeling the total free energy, including lattice and magnetoelastic terms, based on the material's crystal symmetry ($D_{6h}$).
key_observables: Saturated magnetostriction ($\lambda_S$), two-fold symmetry anisotropy in the (0001) plane, Néel transition temperature ($\sim 307$ K), and the spin-flop field ($\sim 0.7$ T).
important_parameters: Elastic coefficients ($c_{11}, c_{12}$), coupling strengths ($\lambda_{1N0}^2, \lambda_{2N0}^2$), and the saturation field ($\sim 0.7$ T).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the saturated magnetostriction is dominated by the spin-flop-related spin reorientation and that the magnetoelastic coupling can be described by symmetry-allowed terms.
figures_summary: Figures detail crystal growth, structural characterization (XRD, STEM), magnetic transitions (M vs T, M vs H), and most critically, the angular dependence of the saturated magnetostriction via pole figures.
paper_structure: The paper follows a structure of experimental characterization (synthesis, structure, magnetic transitions) leading to the core measurement of magnetostriction, which is then theoretically explained using DFT and Landau theory to explain the observed saturation and anisotropy.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the study's rigorous coupling of electronic structure (DFT) to macroscopic mechanical response (magnetostriction) provides a model for understanding coupled order parameters, which has parallels in quantum materials research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
